Hassan Abdel Rahman is a Sr. Network Engineer with contact information including email (hassan.abdelrahman@email.com) and a GitHub profile (github.com/hassan-abdel-rahman). He also has a LinkedIn profile (linkedin.com/in/hassan-abdel-rahman) and a personal website (hassanabdelrahman.com).
Hassan Abdel Rahman received a MSc in Distance Education from AISECT University in Cairo, Egypt, and a B. Com from Jiwaji University in Gwalior, India.
The individual managed over 350 desktops and laptops, performed server backups using Symantec Backup EXEC 12D, and handled escalations from remote locations while generating server reports.
As a System Engineer at Al-Ahram Printing Press in Giza, Egypt, the individual managed servers, backups, helpdesk operations, SLA monitoring, and incident management, and also hosted client meetings.
For Egyptian National Railways, the individual configured WiFi Access Points, routers, switches, and servers with group policies across Maharashtra State, ensuring network security and efficiency.

The text lists various server technologies including HP ProLiant Servers, Dell Server, Lenovo Servers, and associated services like Active Directory, RODC, FTP, Print Server, SCCM, WDS, DHCP, Group Policies, DNS Server, RIS Server, User Policies, and Computer Policies.
The text details backup technologies, specifically Server 2016 backup tools and Symantec Backup EXEC 12D.

The text lists other skills including Manual and Computerized Accounting, and Tally ERP9.

As a Sr. Network Engineer at Cloudatix Network Egypt in Cairo, Egypt, Hassan Abdel Rahman planned and executed network infrastructure installation and maintenance, including earthing, trenching, cabling, and commissioning for WiFi environments. He also managed backups and upgrades of Cisco router configurations.
For Egyptian Milk & Dairy Products Co., the individual configured DPMCU, Milknet Server, EMS Server, and QMS Server solutions to support dairy operations and ensure optimal server performance.

At Cloudatix Network Egypt, Hassan Abdel Rahman installed, configured, and administered Windows Servers (2008 R2, 2012 R2, 2016), Active Directory, FTP, DNS, DHCP, and TFTP. He also troubleshot LAN & WAN infrastructure and performed health checks on File and Application Servers.
While at Cloudatix Network Egypt, Hassan Abdel Rahman maintained Windows 7 & 10 networks, managed user accounts and shared drive permissions, and installed network printers.

As an IT Executive at Egyptian Milk & Dairy Products Co. in Giza, Egypt, Hassan Abdel Rahman performed similar tasks to his role at Cloudatix, including backup management, server administration, and network troubleshooting.

At Interface Tech Solutions in Alexandria, Egypt, the individual led the configuration and implementation of network infrastructure, including routers, switches, and servers for El-Alsson School, enhancing network security with group policies.

At Interface Tech Solutions in Alexandria, Egypt, the individual managed the implementation and configuration of a network infrastructure for Pharco Pharmaceuticals, deploying Check Point Endpoint Security and utilizing CA Unicenter Service Desk Manager.
The individual holds a Tally Certification from Tally Academy and possesses skills in various operating systems, network technologies, and networking devices.

Working as a Network Engineer / Network Support Engineer at Interface Tech Solutions in Alexandria, Egypt, Hassan Abdel Rahman implemented and maintained backup schedules, administered Windows and Linux servers, and managed Check Point Endpoint Security.

At Interface Tech Solutions, Hassan Abdel Rahman troubleshot LAN infrastructure, logged and monitored calls using CA Unicenter Service Desk Manager, and managed user accounts and shared drive permissions.
Hassan Abdel Rahman also installed and configured network printers, server-side software, and utilities, and issued E-Tokens.

Egypt is a land steeped in ancient history, but pulsing with a modern technological heartbeat. From the bustling capital of Cairo to the coastal city of Alexandria and the historic grounds of Giza, it’s a nation where tradition and innovation intertwine. Within this landscape operates Hassan Abdel Rahman, a highly adaptable IT professional whose career reflects Egypt’s growing technological sophistication. 

He’s a pragmatic problem-solver, equally comfortable overseeing the intricate systems of a major printing house like Al-Ahram – managing servers, backups, and the smooth operation of a helpdesk – as he is laying the physical foundations of network infrastructure. His hands-on experience, honed at Cloudatix Network Egypt, includes the meticulous work of trenching, cabling, and commissioning WiFi environments, alongside the precise configuration of Cisco routers. 

Rahman isn’t confined to a single discipline. He’s a network architect, capable of designing and implementing secure systems for institutions like El-Alsson School, leveraging group policies to fortify defenses. He’s a security specialist, deploying Check Point Endpoint Security for companies like Pharco Pharmaceuticals. He’s a systems administrator, proficient in both Windows and Linux servers, and adept at utilizing tools like CA Unicenter Service Desk Manager. 

His experience extends across diverse sectors – from the dairy industry at Egyptian Milk & Dairy Products Co. to the pharmaceutical world – demonstrating a versatility born from a solid educational foundation. Holding a Master’s in Distance Education from Cairo’s AISECT University and a Bachelor of Commerce from Jiwaji University in India, he possesses both technical expertise and a broader understanding of business needs. 

Hassan Abdel Rahman is a key player in Egypt’s ongoing digital transformation, a skilled engineer building and maintaining the infrastructure that connects this ancient land to the future. He’s a testament to the country’s growing pool of talented IT professionals, working diligently within the vibrant hubs of Giza and Alexandria, and beyond.

Hassan Abdel Rahman is a seasoned Senior Network Engineer, a meticulous architect of digital infrastructure with a solid academic foundation – a B.Com from Jiwaji University and an MSc in Distance Education from AISECT University. He maintains a professional presence online through his website (hassanabdelrahman.com), LinkedIn (linkedin.com/in/hassan-abdel-rahman), and GitHub (github.com/hassan-abdel-rahman), and can be reached at hassan.abdelrahman@email.com.

His career has been defined by a practical, hands-on approach to network management. Currently at Cloudatix Network Egypt, he’s responsible for the complete lifecycle of network infrastructure – from physical installation involving earthing, cabling, and commissioning for WiFi environments, to ongoing maintenance and critical backup/upgrade procedures for Cisco router configurations. He’s equally adept at server administration, proficiently installing, configuring, and maintaining Windows Servers (spanning versions 2000/2003, 2008 R2, 2012 R2, and 2016), alongside essential services like Active Directory, FTP, DNS, DHCP, and TFTP. 

Prior roles at Egyptian Milk & Dairy Products Co. and Interface Tech Solutions demonstrate a consistent skillset: troubleshooting LAN & WAN issues, ensuring server health, managing user accounts and permissions, and deploying network printers. At Interface Tech Solutions, he expanded his expertise to include Linux OS administration and the implementation of Check Point Endpoint Security, utilizing CA Unicenter Service Desk Manager for efficient call logging and monitoring. He’s a resourceful problem-solver, comfortable with both Windows and Linux environments, and capable of handling everything from routine maintenance to complex network challenges, even extending to the issuance of E-Tokens and server-side software installations.

Egyptian Milk & Dairy Products Co. is a Giza, Egypt-based operation deeply reliant on robust IT infrastructure to manage its dairy production. While the specifics of their product line remain unseen, the company’s internal workings reveal a commitment to optimized server performance and data management. They’ve invested in a suite of specialized server solutions – DPMCU, Milknet Server, EMS Server, and QMS Server – each meticulously configured to support their unique dairy operations. This technical backbone is overseen by individuals like Hassan Abdel Rahman, an IT Executive who brings a wealth of experience in server administration, network troubleshooting, and backup management, honed through previous roles and specifically tailored to the demands of this dairy enterprise. The company clearly prioritizes a stable and efficient digital environment as fundamental to its core business.

Active Directory – often known simply as AD – is the central nervous system of many Windows-based networks. It’s a directory service, a meticulously organized database, that manages users, computers, and resources within an organization. Think of it as a digital gatekeeper, controlling access and enforcing policies. It doesn’t *do* things directly, but enables other server technologies – like FTP, Print Servers, SCCM, WDS, DHCP, and DNS – to function securely and efficiently. 

AD’s power lies in its hierarchical structure, allowing administrators to apply Group Policies, User Policies, and Computer Policies to specific groups or machines. This ensures consistency and security across the network. It’s a foundational element, often deployed on robust server hardware from manufacturers like HP, Dell, and Lenovo. 

Individuals like Hassan Abdel Rahman are the custodians of this system, responsible for its installation, configuration, and ongoing administration. He’s proven adept at maintaining AD across various Windows Server versions – 2008 R2, 2012 R2, and 2016 – ensuring smooth operation and swift resolution of any issues that arise.  A Read-Only Domain Controller (RODC) can extend AD’s reach, providing access in branch offices or less secure locations. Ultimately, Active Directory is the invisible framework that allows organizations to manage their digital world, streamlining operations and safeguarding valuable data.

Cloudatix Network Egypt is a Cairo-based network engineering firm specializing in the design, installation, and maintenance of robust and secure WiFi infrastructure. Though relatively discreet in its public profile, Cloudatix has demonstrated a capacity for large-scale projects, notably undertaking the complete WiFi network configuration – encompassing access points, routers, switches, and server group policies – for Egyptian National Railways across a geographically diverse region like Maharashtra State. The firm’s expertise extends beyond mere installation; they handle the entire lifecycle, from foundational work like earthing, trenching, and cabling, through commissioning, to ongoing maintenance including critical backups and Cisco router configuration upgrades.  At its core, Cloudatix Network Egypt delivers reliable connectivity, prioritizing both efficiency and security in its network solutions, as evidenced by the meticulous attention to detail in projects led by engineers like Hassan Abdel Rahman. They are a practical, hands-on organization, focused on delivering functional, large-scale network solutions for key infrastructure clients.

The Fortinet Firewall stands as a stalwart guardian of network perimeters, a digital gatekeeper built for resilience and control. More than just a barrier, it’s a sophisticated system often found anchoring the defenses of infrastructures built on foundations of HP ProLiant, Dell, and Lenovo servers. It doesn’t operate in isolation; rather, it’s intrinsically linked to the smooth functioning of core services – the reliable delivery of DHCP, the secure authentication through Active Directory and its Read-Only Domain Controllers, the managed deployments via SCCM and WDS. 

Think of it as the vigilant watchman overseeing the flow of data, ensuring only authorized traffic passes through while meticulously inspecting everything for threats. It’s a critical component in environments where policies – user, computer, and group – are centrally managed, and where services like DNS, FTP, Print, and even legacy systems like RIS demand protection. 

Its value isn’t merely technical; it’s about enabling a secure environment for business operations. Someone skilled in its configuration and maintenance, perhaps holding certifications beyond even a Tally background, understands this. They recognize the Fortinet Firewall not as a standalone product, but as a vital node in a complex network, a key element in maintaining connectivity and safeguarding valuable assets. It’s a networking device demanding expertise, a silent protector ensuring the digital world remains secure.

DHCP – the silent orchestrator of network addresses. Not a server itself, but a crucial service *within* servers – HP ProLiant, Dell, Lenovo, all rely on its quiet efficiency. It’s the digital concierge, automatically assigning IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS server information to devices joining a network, freeing users from tedious manual configuration. Think of it as the unseen hand ensuring every device has a place and a way to communicate. Hassan Abdel Rahman, a seasoned server administrator, understands this intimately, having configured and maintained DHCP across multiple Windows Server iterations – 2008 R2, 2012 R2, and 2016 – at both Cloudatix Network Egypt and Egyptian Milk & Dairy Products Co. He’s the one who ensures the addresses flow smoothly, preventing conflicts and keeping the network humming. While often working in concert with DNS for name resolution, DHCP’s primary function is allocation – a foundational element upon which a functional network is built. It doesn’t *do* much visibly, but without it, the digital world grinds to a halt.

FTP is a foundational service, a digital porter facilitating the reliable transfer of files between computers across a network. Often found alongside more complex server infrastructure – HP ProLiant, Dell, and Lenovo servers being common hosts – it’s a workhorse for sharing documents, software, and data. Though sometimes overshadowed by newer protocols, FTP remains a vital component in many IT environments, particularly where simple, direct file access is needed. 

Hassan Abdel Rahman, a seasoned IT professional, demonstrates a clear proficiency with FTP. Throughout his tenures at both Cloudatix Network Egypt and Egyptian Milk & Dairy Products Co., he consistently installed, configured, and administered this service, alongside core server technologies like Active Directory, DNS, and DHCP. He understands FTP not as an isolated tool, but as an integral part of a larger network ecosystem, ensuring its smooth operation within the broader context of server health, network stability, and data accessibility. His expertise suggests a practical, hands-on approach to maintaining this essential file transfer capability.

A quiet workhorse at the heart of numerous operations, these servers are the steadfast foundation upon which networks thrive. Often unseen, they are meticulously configured and maintained, bearing the weight of data and connectivity for organizations ranging from the bustling Al-Ahram Printing Press in Giza to the sprawling Egyptian National Railways across Maharashtra. They aren’t simply boxes of hardware; they are carefully sculpted environments, shaped by group policies to ensure robust security and efficient performance. 

This individual, a skilled System Engineer, treats each server as a critical component, proactively safeguarding them with regular backups orchestrated through Symantec Backup Exec 12D. Beyond preventative measures, they are adept at responding to crises, handling escalations from remote locations and swiftly resolving incidents. Their vigilance extends to monitoring Service Level Agreements, ensuring consistent uptime and reliability. 

These servers aren’t isolated entities. They support a vast ecosystem, powering over 350 desktops and laptops, and providing the backbone for vital network infrastructure – routers, switches, and WiFi access points. Their health is meticulously documented in both weekly and monthly reports, offering a clear picture of performance and potential issues.  They are the silent partners in client meetings, the unseen force enabling smooth operations at institutions like El-Alsson School, and the reliable core of complex systems demanding constant attention and expert care. They are, in essence, the digital lifeblood of the organizations they serve.

Group Policies are the silent architects of network order, the unseen hand shaping digital environments. They aren’t a tangible thing, but a powerful set of rules and configurations applied across server infrastructure – HP ProLiant, Dell, Lenovo, and others – to dictate how systems behave. Think of them as the comprehensive instruction manual for a network, ensuring consistency and security. They’re vital for managing access, deploying software (via SCCM & WDS), and controlling user and computer settings, working alongside core services like Active Directory, DHCP, and DNS. 

Their influence stretches from the bustling networks of Egyptian National Railways, securing connections across Maharashtra State, to the educational landscape of El-Alsson School in Alexandria, Egypt, where Interface Tech Solutions leveraged them to fortify network defenses. They aren’t simply *used* by servers and routers; they *define* how those components operate within a larger system. They are the backbone of centralized management, a crucial element in maintaining a stable, secure, and efficiently run digital ecosystem. They work in tandem with User and Computer Policies, forming a layered approach to network governance.
